description: Visual puzzles and cognitive traps for the Fake Majority game. Each question has a clearly correct answer that social pressure can override. Admin picks one per game run — system can also pick randomly.

questions.question: Which line is longer — A or B?
questions.explanation: Both lines are exactly the same length. The inward-pointing arrows on B make it appear shorter. This is the Müller-Lyer illusion — one of the most famous in psychology.
questions.infiltratorScript: It's obviously B — look at how much space it takes up. B is clearly longer.
questions.psychologyNote: Even people who know this illusion still perceive B as shorter. Very effective for social pressure.

questions.question: Which orange circle is bigger — A or B?
questions.explanation: Both orange circles are identical in size. Circle A appears smaller because it is surrounded by large grey circles. Circle B appears bigger because it is surrounded by small grey circles. This is the Ebbinghaus illusion.
questions.infiltratorScript: B is bigger — I can literally see it. B is way larger than A.
questions.psychologyNote: Extremely effective. Almost everyone perceives B as larger on first glance. Confidence trap works very well here.

questions.question: What is 2 + 2 × 3?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is 8. Multiplication is done before addition — BODMAS/PEMDAS rule. 2 × 3 = 6, then 2 + 6 = 8. Many people do it left to right and get 12.
questions.infiltratorScript: It's 12 — you always go left to right in basic maths. Simple. 2+2 is 4, 4×3 is 12.
questions.psychologyNote: This one is extremely effective at a tech fest — engineering students know BODMAS but under pressure and with loud confidence from infiltrators, many switch to 12.

questions.question: Which line is longer — the red vertical or the white horizontal?
questions.explanation: Both lines are exactly the same length. Vertical lines consistently appear longer than horizontal lines of equal length. This is the horizontal-vertical illusion.
questions.infiltratorScript: The white horizontal line is clearly longer — it goes all the way across. B is obviously right.
questions.psychologyNote: The white line visually dominates the composition so it feels longer. Works very well.

questions.question: Are squares A and B the exact same colour?
questions.optionA: Yes — they are the same colour
questions.explanation: Squares A and B are both exactly the same shade of grey (#888888). The shadow cast by the cylinder makes B appear much lighter. This is the checker shadow illusion — considered one of the strongest illusions ever discovered.
questions.infiltratorScript: No way they're the same. B is light grey, A is dark grey. Obviously different. Anyone who says same is overthinking it.
questions.psychologyNote: This is brutally effective. Even after being told they are the same shade, the brain refuses to believe it. Highest elimination rate of any question.

questions.question: Which yellow bar is longer — A (top) or B (bottom)?
questions.explanation: Both yellow bars are identical in length. The converging lines (like railway tracks disappearing to a vanishing point) make the brain perceive the upper bar as being further away — and therefore as larger. This is the Ponzo illusion.
questions.infiltratorScript: B is longer — look at how wide it appears compared to A. B takes up more space.
questions.psychologyNote: Moderate effectiveness. People who know perspective tricks may see through it faster. Good mid-event question.

questions.question: How many letter F's are in the passage? (read once only)
questions.explanation: There are 6 F's. Most people count 3 — they miss the F's in the word 'OF' (three times). The brain processes 'of' as a function word and skips scanning it. This is a famous test of selective attention.
questions.infiltratorScript: Three. Finished, Files, Scientific. Three F's. Anyone saying 6 is counting wrong — re-read it.
questions.psychologyNote: WARNING: This requires players to read text, not just see an image. Show it for slightly longer (5–6 seconds). Very powerful — the 'right-seeming' wrong answer is 3. Most educated people confidently get this wrong.

questions.question: Which inner square is darker — A (on black) or B (on white)?
questions.optionA: A is darker (on the black background)
questions.optionB: B is darker (on the white background)
questions.explanation: Both inner squares are the exact same shade of grey. The black background makes A appear lighter, and the white background makes B appear darker. This is simultaneous brightness contrast.
questions.infiltratorScript: B is obviously darker — you can see the grey is much deeper on the right side. Clear answer.
questions.psychologyNote: Effective but slightly less powerful than q5. Good as a warm-up or alternate question.

questions.question: Are the four red lines parallel to each other?
questions.explanation: All four red lines are perfectly parallel. The short diagonal lines crossing them create an illusion of tilt — the lines appear to converge and diverge. This is the Zöllner illusion.
questions.infiltratorScript: They are definitely NOT parallel — look at them, they're clearly tilting. The top and bottom lines clearly lean away from each other.
questions.psychologyNote: Simpler question — good for a round where you want fewer eliminations. Most people get this right eventually but pressure can flip them.

questions.question: How many squares are in a 4×4 grid — counting ALL sizes?
questions.explanation: The answer is 30 total squares. 16 × 1×1 squares + 9 × 2×2 + 4 × 3×3 + 1 × 4×4 = 30. Most people only count the small unit squares (16) or lose track. Option C (30) is correct but looks largest and feels suspicious.
questions.infiltratorScript: It's 16 — you count the boxes on the grid. 4 by 4, that's 16. Simple. Anyone saying 30 is making up squares.
questions.adminNote: This question has 3 options (A:16, B:20, C:30). C is correct. In the admin panel, set correct answer to C. The fake majority should be biased toward A (16) — most people's instinctive wrong answer.

questions.question: Which side has more dots — A (white, small) or B (red, large)?
questions.optionA: A has more (16 small white dots)
questions.optionB: B has more (13 large red dots)
questions.explanation: Side A has 16 dots. Side B has 13 dots. However, the red dots on side B are much larger, making that side look 'fuller' and more numerous at a glance. This exploits how the brain estimates quantity by area rather than count.
questions.infiltratorScript: B has more — look at how full the right side is. It's obviously more packed. A is spread out and sparse.
questions.psychologyNote: Very effective. Large objects feel like 'more' even when there are fewer. Works best with the fast flash (3 seconds) so players cannot count.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger — 3/7 or 4/9?
questions.explanation: 3/7 ≈ 0.4286 is larger than 4/9 ≈ 0.4444... wait — actually 4/9 is larger. 4/9 = 0.444 vs 3/7 = 0.4286. Correct answer is B. The larger numerator AND larger denominator of B makes this genuinely confusing.

4/9 ≈ 0.444 is larger than 3/7 ≈ 0.429. The trick is that both the numerator and denominator of B are larger, which makes your brain say '3/7 is simpler so it must be bigger'. This is a genuine cognitive trap — not just an illusion.
questions.infiltratorScript: 3/7 is bigger — smaller numbers, simpler fraction, closer to a half. Obviously A.
questions.psychologyNote: Works extremely well at a tech fest. Students know fractions but the time pressure and wrong-intuition combine powerfully.

questions.question: What did you see FIRST when the image appeared?
questions.explanation: This is a trick question — there is no single correct answer. The image can be seen as either a duck (beak pointing left) or a rabbit (ears pointing left). The 'correct answer' is set to A (duck) but the game admin can choose either. The point is to create disagreement and social pressure.
questions.infiltratorScript: It's a rabbit — obviously a rabbit. Anyone who sees a duck is seeing it wrong. The ears are right there.
questions.adminNote: This question has no objectively correct answer — set whichever you want as correct in the admin panel. The fun is that both answers are defensible, so the infiltrators can argue either side convincingly. Great for creating room debate.

questions.question: Is there really a missing ₹1?
questions.optionB: No — the maths is just framed wrong
questions.explanation: No rupee is missing. The trick is adding ₹27 + ₹2 — which is meaningless. The correct accounting is: ₹25 (hotel) + ₹2 (bellboy) + ₹3 (returned) = ₹30. The puzzle forces you to add numbers that should not be added together. The missing rupee does not exist.
questions.infiltratorScript: The rupee is definitely missing. ₹27 + ₹2 = ₹29. That's only ₹29 of ₹30. One rupee is gone. It's basic maths.
questions.psychologyNote: Extremely effective logic trap. The framing feels so confident that even people who sense something is wrong often vote Yes under pressure. Works especially well with the confidence trap — people who say 'yes' at 100% confidence are always wrong.

questions.question: The spiral has two colours — green and blue. True or false?
questions.optionB: False — they are the same colour
questions.explanation: The spiral genuinely does contain two different colours — green (#1a9e75) and blue (#378add). However, because the stripes overlap and create a swirling pattern, many people perceive them as a single colour or cannot distinguish them. This tests colour perception under visual complexity.
questions.infiltratorScript: They're the same colour. It's all the same shade. Anyone saying they see two colours is imagining it — just one colour.
questions.psychologyNote: This works because the overlapping spiral pattern makes colour distinction genuinely difficult even though the colours are different. Medium effectiveness — use when you want a closer split.

adminNotes.usage: In the admin panel, when configuring the Pluralistic Silence game slot, you will see a question picker. Upload this JSON file once and all 15 questions become available. The game can pick randomly or admin selects manually per round.
